[[BLOCK:slide0_shape0]]
Limitations of OLS Regression

[[BLOCK:slide0_shape1]]
Chong Ho Yu

[[BLOCK:slide1_shape0]]
Historical Context of OLS Regression

[[BLOCK:slide1_shape1]]
Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression, also referred to as Standard Least Squares (SLS) regression, was introduced by Legendre (1805) and Gauss (1809), dating back to the era when our great grandparents were born.

[[BLOCK:slide2_shape0]]
OLS Regression

[[BLOCK:slide2_shape1]]
Least squares = minimizing the square of Residuals (Residuals)
Residual = the difference between observed and predicted values
Objective: Best fit

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape0]]
R Square

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape1]]
The goal of simple regression is to identify a relationship.  
For multiple predictors, the combined relationship is represented by the R Square, which indicates the proportion of variance explained by the model.

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape2_shape0]]
Y

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape2_shape1]]
X

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape2_shape2]]
R Squared

[[BLOCK:slide4_shape0]]
Ideal Conditions

[[BLOCK:slide4_shape1]]
All predictors are uncorrelated (orthogonal).

[[BLOCK:slide5_shape0]]
Overstated Variance Explained

[[BLOCK:slide5_shape1]]
Imagine the overlap between Y and Xs as the variance explained (multiple-relationship).  
As more predictors (Xs) are added to Y, the circle representing Y becomes nearly fully covered.  
R Square = 0.89! Impressive! But is it meaningful?

[[BLOCK:slide6_shape0]]
An Ineffective Model

[[BLOCK:slide6_shape1]]
A student once asked how to improve his grade. I explained that my fifty-variable regression model could predict nearly 89% of test performance: study longer, earn more money, buy a reliable car, watch less TV, browse the Web more, exercise regularly, attend church frequently, pray often, go to fewer movies, play fewer video games, cut your hair more often, drink more milk and coffee, etc.  
This overly complex model is impractical and ineffective!

[[BLOCK:slide7_shape0]]
Stepwise Regression

[[BLOCK:slide7_shape1]]
Lavery et al. (2016): “Older methods (e.g., bivariate screening, stepwise selection, forward selection) are flawed. The statistics provided in regression outputs (p-values, parameter estimates, R2, etc.) were designed for testing a single model and are not valid when repeatedly used as in stepwise regression” (p.1).

[[BLOCK:slide8_shape0]]
Stepwise Regression

[[BLOCK:slide8_shape1]]
Smith (2018): “Many Big Data researchers assume that having more explanatory variables makes stepwise regression more effective for variable selection. In reality, stepwise regression becomes less effective as the number of potential explanatory variables increases. It does not address the Big Data issue of excessive explanatory variables. Instead, Big Data amplifies the shortcomings of stepwise regression.”  
https://doi.org/10.1186/s40537-018-0143-6

[[BLOCK:slide8_note]]
Smith, G. (2018). Step away from stepwise. Journal of Big Data, 5, 1-12. https://doi.org/10.1186/s40537-018-0143-6.

[[BLOCK:slide9_shape0]]
Fitness Example

[[BLOCK:slide9_shape1]]
In this example, six variables from fitness data are used to predict weight. The method employed is OLS regression.

[[BLOCK:slide10_shape0]]
Negative Adjusted R Square!

[[BLOCK:slide10_shape1]]
The R Square is 0.199—not bad! This model explains 20% of the variance in weight.  
However, when many predictors are included, the program adjusts the inflated R Square using adjusted R Square.  
The adjusted R Square is negative! Since R Square is r * r, it should not be negative. What is happening?

[[BLOCK:slide11_shape0]]
Negative R Square Explained

[[BLOCK:slide11_shape1]]
Zero indicates no variance explained.  
If a line is fitted to the data and it is flat (horizontal), the model explains no variance.  
If the model performs worse than a horizontal line and contradicts the data pattern, the adjusted R Square can be negative.  
This occurs when too many predictors are included, making the model worse than having no model at all.  
Metaphor: Taking the wrong or fake medicine when sick is worse than taking no medicine.

[[BLOCK:slide12_shape0]]
Challenges of OLS Regression

[[BLOCK:slide12_shape1]]
- Excessive assumptions about Residuals (Residuals) and predictors  
- Prone to overfitting  
- Model instability when predictors are highly correlated (Multi-collinearity (Multi-collinearity))  
- No unique solution for large datasets  
- Conventional rule of thumb: 1 predictor per 10 observations; OLS regression is infeasible if predictors outnumber observations (p > n).  
- Requires a linear model.

[[BLOCK:slide13_shape0]]
Generalized Regression

[[BLOCK:slide13_shape1]]
Introduced by Friedman (2008)  
Similar to abduction or inference to the best explanation (IBE): avoid fixating on a single answer; consider multiple possibilities.  
There are often several solutions to a problem—why not explore them?  
Start with no modeling or zero coefficients.  
Experiment with a series of models. The solution is flexible (adaptive).  
Select the best model (determined by the algorithm, not manually).

[[BLOCK:slide14_shape0]]
KISS Principle

[[BLOCK:slide14_shape1]]
Also referred to as Regularized Regression (Regularized Regression): aims to reduce variance and enhance the model's generalizability, thereby preventing overfitting.  
Also known as penalized regression: imposes a penalty on overly complex models or inflated fitness → Keep It Simple, Stupid (KISS)!

[[BLOCK:slide15_shape0]]
Loss Function

[[BLOCK:slide15_shape1]]
A Loss Function (Loss Function) maps one or more variables to a real number, representing the cost of an event.  
What is the cost of making a mistake in an event? What is lost?  
In statistics, the cost is the error or Residual (Residual).

[[BLOCK:slide16_shape0]]
Loss Function: A Metaphor

[[BLOCK:slide16_shape1]]
Imagine a hiker descending a mountain at dusk. He scans the environment for possible paths. Upon realizing a chosen trail does not lead to the destination, he abandons it due to the high cost in time and energy. After several trials and errors, he eliminates poor choices and moves toward paths with decreasing loss. Eventually, he selects the most cost-effective route, minimizing loss.

[[BLOCK:slide18_shape0]]
Methods of Regularization and Penalization

[[BLOCK:slide19_shape0]]
Regularization Techniques

[[BLOCK:slide19_shape1]]
The primary distinction between OLS and penalized regression lies in the addition of a regularization term to the Loss Function (Loss Function) in penalized regression, optimizing coefficient estimation. Thus, penalized regression is also referred to as Regularized Regression (Regularized Regression).  
- L1 regularization (LASSO): adds the sum of absolute coefficient estimates as the regularization term.  
- L2 regularization (Ridge): adds the sum of squared coefficient estimates.
